stem: What is the average value of \( f(x) = ([[p]])x^{2} + ([[q]])x + ([[r]]) \) on the interval \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the value of the integral itself, without dividing by the length of the interval.
This evaluates \( f \) at the midpoint of the interval instead of averaging it.
This averages only the two endpoint values of \( f \), which is not the average value of a nonlinear function.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = ([[A]])\sin([[k]]x) + ([[B]]) \). What is the average value of \( f \) over the interval \( \left[ [[c]],\ [[c]] + \dfrac{2\pi}{[[k]]} \right] \), i.e. over exactly one full period of the oscillation?
solution: Over exactly one full period, the oscillating part \( ([[A]])\sin([[k]]x) \) contributes zero net area (equal area above and below the shift), so the average value equals just the vertical shift \( [[B]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the amplitude to the shift as if the sine term contributed its maximum on average.
This adds half the amplitude, a common but incorrect guess for the 'average' of a sine wave.
This wrongly assumes the whole average vanishes, ignoring the vertical shift \( [[B]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: What is the average value of \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[A]]}{x} \) on the interval \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the value of the integral itself, forgetting to divide by the width \( [[q]]-[[p]] \).
This is the antiderivative difference written in ratio form but still not divided by the interval width.
This evaluates \( f \) at the geometric-mean-like product of the endpoints instead of averaging.
This uses the average of \( 1/x \) evaluated at the endpoints (harmonic-mean style) rather than the true average value.
This is half the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A particle moves along a line with velocity \( v(t) = ([[p]])t^{2} + ([[q]])t + ([[r]]) \) meters per second, for \( t \) in seconds. What is the particle's average velocity over the time interval \( [[[t0]], [[t1]]] \)?
solution: Average velocity is total displacement divided by elapsed time: \( \frac{1}{[[t1]]-[[t0]]}\int_{[[t0]]}^{[[t1]]} v(t)\,dt = [[answer]] \) m/s.
distractor_rationales: This is the total displacement (the integral itself), without dividing by the elapsed time.
This averages the two endpoint velocities, which is not the true average value of a nonlinear velocity function.
This computes the net change in velocity \( v([[t1]]) - v([[t0]]) \) rather than the average velocity.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: For \( f(x) = x^{2} + c \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), for what value of the constant \( c \) does the average value of \( f \) on this interval equal \( [[target]] \)?
solution: The average value is \( \frac{1}{[[b]]-[[a]]}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}(x^{2}+c)\,dx = \frac{[[b]]^{2}+[[a]][[b]]+[[a]]^{2}}{3} + c \). Setting this equal to \( [[target]] \) and solving gives \( c = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the average of the squared endpoints instead of the correct average of \( x^{2} \), which is \( \frac{a^{2}+ab+b^{2}}{3} \).
This adds instead of subtracts the (incorrect) average-of-endpoints term.
This uses the square of the midpoint of the endpoints instead of the true average of \( x^{2} \) on the interval.
This forgets to account for the contribution of the \( x^{2} \) term at all.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
